speaker: interviewer
text: Reva, do you remember actually arriving at your aunt's apartment when you were moving in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I do. Yes. Yes, I do remember coming in. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What was that day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very sad day because you had to leave everything behind you, you know? You have friends that you left behind. You left your home. You left your surroundings, your familiar surroundings. You have to move into a place that is going to be crowded. You're not going to be-- you're sort of-- your individuality was taken away from you. You become a group rather than an individual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you remember thinking-- I mean, you were six years old. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if I was thinking it then. Maybe I'm thinking it now. But, but I knew-- I could sense a lot of the things as a little girl. You know, I was, after all, six, seven years old. You can feel a lot of things. And I'm pretty sure I remember feeling that my life is going to be changed from now on. This is it. This is going to be a different way of living. I will have to share a lot of things with other people and sleep on floors rather than on beds, and sleep having your thing under your-- instead of a pillow, having this bag of clothing that you have, and you're constantly going to go from place to place. So it was very, very different, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your sisters? Did they talk much about not being able to go to school anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. When you're young, I suppose-- they still had their friends. I am sure, among them, among their friends, they must have talked about it. I was pretty much protected. I really stuck with my mom all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I really don't remember whether-- I'm sure they must have talked about it, because it had to be very, very hard for them, because their life, too, was disrupted. You know, when you're told you cannot go to school anymore, what do you do it yourself? I mean, you have to find some other alternative, something else to do. What did you and your mother do every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we would go to the markets. You know, the ghetto was pretty-- for, for a while, it was pretty normal yet, in 1940. And I remember going with my mother to the market, to the-- like a farmer's market. And whatever food she could buy, she would buy. And we would come home, and she would cook it, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my life was pretty normal still. You know, it was not much to do, but it was a normal life. She would do the regular things-- wash clothes, try to cook whatever food she had. And we would eat whatever she would serve us, you know. And that was about it. And what did you do at seven? Did you help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I was mostly playing. I was just-- whatever I could find, I would go out into the yard and play with the children. Whatever children they would, you know, be there, I would play with them. We would play hopscotch, you know, regular children's games.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would play with the ball. I had this rubber ball that I played with. And my life actually was pretty normal, you know? Considering under the circumstances I lived, it was a pretty normal life. Were you aware of holidays when they happened? Did you do anything different on holidays?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember that at all. Unh-uh. I don't remember, in the ghetto, having holidays. I only remember the holidays before the ghetto started. During the ghetto, I don't remember any holidays at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any difference on Shabbat and the rest of the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it at all. I don't think so because we did not have the kind of food that we have for Shabbat before the war. We had the rations. We had the bread rations. We had to go in line, stand in line to get the bread. And no, I don't remember it, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it feel like to be hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my goodness. What does it feel like to be hungry? You have a pain in your stomach that never goes away. That's the only thing I can describe. You have a terrible, terrible pain, and there's nothing that you can do. It's just a, a gnawing, is it, pain in your stomach, and it's terrible, so terrible. It's nothing compared when anybody goes on diets and doesn't eat. Believe me, it is a terrible pain just to know that there's no food to eat-- very painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you go between meals when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were days when I had no food at all, just water. You know, there were many days that we had no food. And, and we would save whatever piece of bread we would have. We would save it. We would eat a small little piece at a time, so we would have it the next day. And but we were lucky. In many ways, we were very, very lucky too because my sisters smuggled. They were smugglers, what we called in the ghetto. And they would go into the Aryan side on the street car, and they would go as Polish girls. They would go into the other side and bring in food to-- for the Jewish people in the ghetto. And they were able to sell some of it. And therefore, we were able to eat, too. They would bring in flowers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that? How did they leave? Just get on a street car and leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, smuggled. If they would have been caught, they would have been shot to death. They went as non-Jewish girls, and they were smuggling on the street cars. My two-- three sisters, my sister Hannah, Mindall, and Eda. And they, they spoke polish beautifully. Nobody would detect at all that they were Jewish girls. But they were smuggling, endangering their lives. If the Germans would have caught them, they would have been dead. They would have killed them on the spot. So but they endangered their lives, but they knew that the only way to survive would be to go smuggle and bring food into the ghetto. And that's how we survived, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them get on the streetcar when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. No, I didn't. But I was hidden. My mother kept me pretty much by her side because I was so young, you know. But everybody else saw them, believe me. It was a well-known fact in the ghetto that not only my sisters did, and my brother, but other, other kids did, too. This was the only way of survival. And for a long, long time, they did that. And but I do remember one thing. I remember when they were building the, the wall, the ghetto wall. You know, and I used to stand and look at the people that were working at it. And on top of the brick wall, they put glass on, and wires. So in other words, if somebody would try to go over the wall, they would be cut to shreds. And it was a pretty devastating feeling to see how they were closing you in, you know, squeezing you to death, slowly but surely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your grandmother living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my grandmother was living in her own house with my father's oldest sister, my s-- Auntie Hannah, And she had two children. She was a widow. And my, my Grandma Zlata lived with them. But she lived close enough on the streets where we were able to walk to her house, so we saw her. And we, we saw her, how she died slowly. But at that point, we already ourselves did not have any food to share with her because things were getting very tighter, and the Germans were getting much, much messier. They were more strict. They would not-- you couldn't smuggle that easily anymore. And a lot of people were killed, so people quit doing it because they didn't want to endanger their lives. And slowly, the ghetto became smaller and smaller and smaller because people were being taken out. And everybody was promised they would-- they would get bread to eat if they would go to work. And people, being that they were all hungry, decided to go. They just took their little suitcase, whatever they had, and they went to on the march. And, of course, unfortunately nobody came back. Nobody came back to tell us where they were going till it was too late. And we knew that something was happening because nobody was returning. Till one day, I remember in the ghetto, when they finally came to look for people, and they took away my aunt, the one that we were living with. They took her away with, with her little girl, two-year-old little beautiful little girl. And, of course, she never returned. And then, and slowly, they tried to take away all the people. Eventually, the whole ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: When people were leaving, did you have any sense, as a child, of, of where they were going at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. I had no idea. I heard people talk. And they said that they're taking him away to work. But we know it's better. We know that they're taking him to death, because it is impossible, because none of these people are returning. So they are taking him to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did your mother ever talk to you about what you were going to do next? And, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened, two of my older sisters-- my sister Eda, my sister Mindall went away to work for some Polish farmers. And they went away for quite a while. And then, while they-- while they were working at this farm, they had overheard that the Jews are being killed in Warsaw, that all the Jews are being taken away. At that point, my sisters worked as two non-Jewish girls. They worked as farm help, farm hands. And they wore crosses, and they went to church for mass, and all of that. They just lived as two non-Jewish girls. But one day they had heard that all the Jews in Warsaw are being taken away, and they're all being killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they decided to come back to the ghetto, my two sisters, Eda and Mindall. And when they came back to the ghetto, they had said, we have to get out of here. We cannot stay here because the Germans are going to come after us, and they're going to kill us. We have to go to the Aryan side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was willing to do it. She said, fine, we'll all go-- hope-- hoping, of course, that somebody would save us and they would keep us there. So my sister Ed-- first of all, my mother said, you go first. You go out, you go first, and I will follow you with my other sister, Marsha. And we said, fine, we'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister, Hannah, and my other two sisters left first. And then my sister Eda came after me. She took me. Now, my sister, Eda, is four years older than I am. And she decided to take me. She says, I'm going to take Reva, and we're going to go. So we went. We crossed the wall, hidden, of course. We went underneath the wall. Nobody saw us go because had they seen us, they too would have killed us, the Germans. Underneath the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underneath the wall there were like, rats, you know, holes underneath, behind. So we smuggled ourselves through. We didn't go through the gate because they wouldn't allow you to go through the gate. So we, we crawled under the hole, just like an animal would crawl underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did you dig the hole? Or was the hole--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the holes were there, evidently from other people, probably. But we knew where they were. So we dug ourselves through the holes, and we got into the Aryan side. And all of us were there already, my sisters, and my brother. And we waited, and we waited, and we waited. And my mother never came. This was July, 1942. And my mother and sister were taken away by the Germans. That was one of the last transports in the ghetto. And my mother never came. She was taken away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was taken away with my-- with my sister, Marsha to Treblinka. And, of course, we never heard from them again. But I don't even know if my mother had gone with us, if she would have survived because it was very, very difficult for her to see all her children suffering and everybody else dying around her. We don't even know if she would have survived. We really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reva, how do you know your mother was taken to Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because most of the Jewish people from Warsaw were taken to, to Treblinka. They never returned. And we knew. We knew. I had an uncle, my mother's youngest brother, who was taken on the train, and he jumped off the train because he found out. He was a very young, handsome young man. And he found out that they were taking him to Treblinka, and he jumped off the train. And he was killed. And, of course, nobody returned from Treblinka to tell us that they saw him, but someone did tell us, though, that he did jump off. A lot of people might have done that, too, because they knew where they were going. But Treblinka was the worst place. Nobody returned from Treblinka-- nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, did you know about Treblinka, in July?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know. But you know, I did remember seeing my sister in line, marching with all the women. And my sister sort of pointed to us, not to say anything. She sort of motioned to us not to say anything because we wanted her to get out of the line. We were willing to take her out. And she said, no, no, no, just, you know, motioned not to say anything. And she was standing with my mother. And before you know it, they took her away, and we were left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. No, my sister, not my mother. Because they had separated the young women and the old women. Not that my mother was so old. Like I said, my mother was only 39 years old. But they took them separately. But we did see our sister for the last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're on the other side of the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Aryan side. And we went to a friend that my father had known, thinking that maybe they would save us. And we had given him whatever we had, any valuable things that we had. And they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't tell you in the story before, but after the Germans had evacuated all the people from the ghetto, my sister, Eda, and I went back into the ghetto, and to the apartments where all the people lived. And whatever we could find of value, we were looting all the homes. We'd take silver, kiddish cups, candlesticks, whatever they had, anything of value-- even linen. And we would wrap ourselves around with it, put it into our pockets, and we would go back and try to sell it. So they'd give it to these people and they would hoping-- we would hope that they would keep us safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go crawl under the wall with all these things on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time, we walked right through because the ghetto was pretty much empty. And the Germans, somehow or another, nobody noticed us. I don't know what. Somebody above must have been watching over us because nobody bothered us. We just walked through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe at that point, they thought maybe we were two little Gentile little girls, you know? And they didn't bother us. And we walked through into the Aryan side, where our other sister was there. And we saved-- we gave these, all the things that we had to these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, that same night, this man that we had given everything to him had a little bit too much to drink. And in the middle of the night, he started yelling at us, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Dirty Jews, get out of here. And he started yelling so loud, and he came with a hatchet with us. And we got very, very frightened. So during the middle of the night, my sister-- Hannah, my oldest sister-- grabbed me. And we started running in the middle of the night to a cornfield not far away. And we hid there at night till daylight came. And when daylight came, we decided we had to go someplace because we cannot be in Warsaw anymore because the Polish people did not want to protect us. And we knew we couldn't get back in because the Germans-- either way you were stuck. You were trapped. You were trapped like an animal. On one side, you had the enemy, the Germans, and on the other side you had the other enemy, the Polish people. And we were trapped. But we did decide to go to a small town where my mother originally was born and my father was born, hoping that maybe there would be family there because my mother had four brothers and two sisters in that small town. And my, my grandma and my aunts and uncles still lived there. So we decided to go to that small town. But by way of going, we took a train. We had saved-- we still had some money left with us. So we went on a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was you and?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, my sister, my brother, and my other sister, Eda, all of us. We went on the train. But for me, I did not speak Polish, because I had never started school. I was too young, and I couldn't start school. And I did not speak Polish. All I spoke was Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So while I was sitting on the train among Gestapo, and Nazis, and SS people, I was sitting as a mute. I pretended I was a mute. I did not speak. And for several hours, we're on that train, and I did not open my mouth. My sisters, of course, spoke. They were Pol-- you know, they spoke Polish. And they really-- what the Polish people assumed-- they look like Polish peo-- you know, kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, we arrived to this town, Demblin. And when we got there, we found out that all the Jews were taken into the square. And there was nobody that we could talk to anymore. Everybody was gone. All my aunts had been taken away. There was only one uncle left. And he was the uncle who was married, actually, to my aunt. He was an uncle by marriage. And when we got there, the people didn't want to have anything to do with us. They thought that we were not Jews, and they didn't want to let us in even to that-- to that group. We found out later that all the people were taken to a camp, to a labor-- a labor camp-- slave labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we followed them. And then at night, during the night, we tried to get into garbage containers, where there was garbage inside. And we hid during the night. And then, during the night, my uncle and some other people came out and took us out from these garbage cans, and they brought us into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, he told us that we were his-- you know, his nephews and nieces. And that's how I started-- this is how the first camp started for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what I don't understand is you got off of a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked to the town square.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked to the town square, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people said, you're not Jewish. Stay away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Once we got into the town square-- well, we walked towards the town square. The people thought that we were Polish girls. They did not know that we were Jews. Had they known we were Jews, they probably would have said, you know, there goes another Jew. Take this one away, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we march-- we walked-- and nobody bothered us till we got to the town square. And when we-- we were trying to find our aunts and uncles. And we couldn't find anybody. Only this uncle was left. How many people were there in the town square?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were thousands of people. It wasn't a very big town, but there were several thousands of Jews. And, of course, they, you know, they divided again, the children and the women and the men. They were divided up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But by this time, you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was I eight years old? Eight? '33-- yeah, I was about eight years old. And so we were in this labor-- slave labor camp. But there weren't too many children. There were about, I think, 11 children-- 11 or 12 children. And I think I was one of the oldest at that time. And what they did, the women and the men worked. My sisters went out to work. They built airports. It was a airfield. In that particular town was an airfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name for this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A what?

speaker: interviewer
text: A name for the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No it was just Demblin-- Demblin labor-- slave labor camp. And the women went out to work in the fields and the gardens. And some of them went out to work in the fields. They carried stones to-- for the-- to build airports. And two of my sisters were lucky. They worked in the gardens where they were growing potatoes, and vegetables, and things like this. So they were able to bring, sometimes, in a potato here. But anybody who was ever caught smuggling or bringing in food was shot. And I remember one day, they found a man who had brought-- they smuggled potatoes. He had found potatoes in his shirt. And the next day, they hung him in front of everybody, in front of all the barracks, so people would know that if you're go to do this again, this is going to happen to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. He was hanging in the-- in the camp. And he was hanging for a long, long time. And then, finally, they cut him down. But they, they were pretty strict about things like that. They did not want you to bring any food in. So you got rations. You got a piece of bread. You got a little bit of soup. Until today, I still have a hard time with the soups because I know the soup was just like-- it looked like cabbage, or, or rutabagas, or one of those-- awful. And, and you would almost see little white worms swimming around. And you know, and it was awful. But when you're hungry, you'll eat anything in order to survive. It was a mat-- it was a matter of survival. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it was summer when you first went into this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summer. July of 1942, we came into that camp. And, of course, the summer at the camp was not as bad as the winters. The winters, of course, were terrible. We lived in, in barracks, which were very, very cold. We have no-- one blanket, I think they gave us, straw underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mainly, in order to keep warm, you had your own bodies, you know? You kept close to one another for warmth. And, and in the winter, when they called you out in the morning for the appel-- you know, for the counting of the people-- they would call you 5:00 in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: For whatever they decided to do it, it was extremely cold in the winter time. And you had no clothing on, no coats. Just whatever you had on, that was it. It was very, very harsh, very harsh. The winters in Poland were very, very harsh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reva, what did the barracks smell like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had a horrible odor, just a horrible, awful odor because of the sanitation. There was no sanitation whatsoever. And whatever you had to take showers, they were cold water. You know, there was no soap. And it had a horrible odors, just awful. And all I can remember is the lice and the bed bugs. I mean, all I could-- when I'm sitting here now in my beautiful, clean home, I can just visualize how horrible it was-- you know, the lice and the bed bugs, and all the other roaches, whatever. It was horrible. And I had no shoes. I had-- my feet were wrapped with rags. And as I said, it was very, very cold, and your feet were chapped, and your hands were chapped, and you had these wounds from, from just the cold. It was very, very painful-- very painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you per barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in our barrack they must have hit 100 women. I'm not quite sure, but I would think there must have been about 100 because they had layers. You know, we had three-- one barrack on top of the-- not barrack. What are those called? Bunks-- bunks. I think there were like, three, three bunks at a time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you and your sisters together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together, yeah. We always slept together in the same barr-- in the same bunk. And we sort of kept ourselves warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did women talk about at night in the bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them talked about their families that they had lost. A lot of them had come from Hungary, people from different parts of Europe. And they talked about their loved ones that they had lost. They lost their wives-- I mean mothers, brothers, husbands, most of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they thought, when is it all going to end? Why do we have to suffer like this? Why doesn't come-- somebody come along and save us? I mean, everybody has deserted us. For what reason? Why? Why is this necessary? Why do we have to suffer just because we happen to be Jewish, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot of times we used to sing. We used to sing quietly. And I know that my sisters and I used to talk mainly. We always used to say to one another, we are going to survive. We are going to live. We have to be strong. We got to survive in order to tell this to other people because nobody is going to believe that anything like this happened-- nobody-- unless there is witnesses. And this is exactly why we have to do this. Nobody would have believed this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reva, what songs did you sing in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to sing a lot of Jewish songs. I learned-- I, I learned one little song while I was in the barracks which really signifies the whole war, the whole thing. It's about a little girl who's sitting in a camp in a little corner, hidden. She had lost her mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's asking God, why me? Why did my mother and father taken away from me? Why is the whole world out there, not doing something about it? Why has everybody deserted us? Where is God? Where is the human-- humans? Where are they? Why do they see our suffering like this and nobody's stepping forward to help us? And she said, the only enemy right now that I have is the enemy-- is the sunshine. And that song has always-- it's a little song, but it's always haunted me. Should I sing it for you? It's very short. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It goes like this. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] This song has always lasted with me, and it will always last, till the day I die because it really signifies the meaning what the war was all about-- nobody doing anything about it and letting millions of people die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's break.
